glossary.def: Missing completely at random: the probability of a value being missing is independent of every value, observed or unobserved. The observed rows are then an unbiased subsample, so complete-case analysis is valid (only less efficient).

Missing at random: the probability of missingness depends only on observed data, not on the missing value itself. Despite the name it is not 'random' — it is recoverable by conditioning on the observed predictors, which is what kNN, MICE, and model-based imputation exploit.

Missing not at random: missingness depends on the unobserved value itself (e.g. high earners hide their income because it is high). It cannot be fixed from the data alone and cannot be distinguished from MAR by any statistic on the observed data; it requires an explicit model of the missingness.

Filling every missing entry of a column with the mean of its observed values. Simple and unbiased for the column mean under MCAR, but it shrinks variance by a factor (n−m)/n and erases the column's correlations with other features.

Filling a hole with the (distance-weighted) average of the k most similar rows, where similarity is computed over the columns both rows observe. It preserves local structure and inter-column correlation but needs features on a comparable scale and degrades in high dimensions.

Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (fully conditional specification): iteratively regress each column with missing data on all the others and draw new fills from that conditional model, sweeping until convergence. Run M times and pool with Rubin's rules to carry imputation uncertainty into final standard errors.

Generating M complete datasets with plausibly different fills, analysing each, and combining the results so the disagreement between imputations inflates the reported variance. Unlike single imputation, it does not pretend the filled values are certain.

Adding a binary 'was-this-missing' column alongside an imputed feature so a flexible model can learn whether the fact of missingness is itself predictive — frequently useful under MNAR and with tree-based models.

refs.cite: Rubin, D. B. (1976). Inference and Missing Data. Biometrika 63(3):581–592.
Little, R. J. A. & Rubin, D. B. (2019). Statistical Analysis with Missing Data (3rd ed.). Wiley.
van Buuren, S. (2018). Flexible Imputation of Missing Data (2nd ed.). CRC Press.
Troyanskaya, O. et al. (2001). Missing value estimation methods for DNA microarrays. Bioinformatics 17(6):520–525.
White, I. R., Royston, P. & Wood, A. M. (2011). Multiple imputation using chained equations: Issues and guidance for practice. Statistics in Medicine 30(4):377–399.
scikit-learn developers. Imputation of missing values (User Guide).
